title: When a Prediction Interval Grades Its Own Homework: The Conformal Meta-Analysis Coverage Advantage Is a Circular-Evaluation Artifact
author: Mahmood Ahmad
summary: Honest out-of-sample evaluation reverses the claim: standard prediction intervals cover 91.7% vs 85.7% for conformal across 365 Cochrane reviews; the earlier conformal advantage was an in-sample artifact.
body: Do conformal prediction intervals outperform standard prediction intervals in meta-analysis, or was their reported advantage an artifact of measurement? We re-examined 365 Cochrane reviews from the Pairwise70 corpus, each with at least five primary-outcome studies, plus a known-truth simulation over several non-normal random-effects laws. The original evaluation built one interval from all studies and counted how many fell inside it, grading conformal on residuals it calibrated on; we instead used honest leave-one-out and known-truth out-of-sample coverage. Out-of-sample, mean coverage was 91.7% for the standard interval, 91.0% for HKSJ, and only 85.7% for conformal, reversing the reported ranking. The simulation agreed: the standard interval stayed near nominal across all random-effects laws while conformal under-covered, worst at five studies, with comparable not threefold widths. The apparent superiority of conformal prediction was a circular in-sample artifact, and the standard interval's t-multiplier supplies small-sample protection. Conformal prediction may still help under heavy contamination or many studies, regimes not examined here.

sentences.text: Do conformal prediction intervals outperform standard prediction intervals in meta-analysis, or was their reported advantage an artifact of measurement?
We re-examined 365 Cochrane reviews from the Pairwise70 corpus, each with at least five primary-outcome studies, plus a known-truth simulation over several non-normal random-effects laws.

The original evaluation built one interval from all studies and counted how many fell inside it, grading conformal on residuals it calibrated on; we instead used honest leave-one-out and known-truth out-of-sample coverage.
Out-of-sample, mean coverage was 91.7% for the standard interval, 91.0% for HKSJ, and only 85.7% for conformal, reversing the reported ranking.
The simulation agreed: the standard interval stayed near nominal across all random-effects laws while conformal under-covered, worst at five studies, with comparable not threefold widths.
The apparent superiority of conformal prediction was a circular in-sample artifact, and the standard interval's t-multiplier supplies small-sample protection.
Conformal prediction may still help under heavy contamination or many studies, regimes not examined here.

notes.validation: DerSimonian-Laird core validated against R metafor to <=3e-16; honest LOO + known-truth simulation; negative result

references: Borenstein M, Hedges LV, Higgins JPT, Rothstein HR. Introduction to Meta-Analysis. 2nd ed. Wiley; 2021.
Higgins JPT, Thompson SG, Deeks JJ, Altman DG. Measuring inconsistency in meta-analyses. BMJ. 2003;327(7414):557-560.
Cochrane Handbook for Systematic Reviews of Interventions. Version 6.4. Cochrane; 2023.
